title: Garage Door Repair and Installation Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Garage Door Repair Business — Costs, Earnings, Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a garage door repair and installation business: real startup costs, earnings, the spring-tension safety risk, and what operators get wrong.
bestFor: Mechanically capable people who can learn precise, safety-critical repair work and like fast same-day jobs
biggestRisk: Mishandling torsion spring tension, which can cause serious injury and is the part most likely to hurt an undertrained operator
whatItIs: A garage door repair and installation business fixes and replaces the parts that make residential and light-commercial garage doors work — broken torsion and extension springs, snapped cables, worn rollers and hinges, off-track doors, dented panels, and malfunctioning openers — and installs new doors and openers. It is a high-volume, high-margin service trade because failures are common, urgent, and something most homeowners will not attempt themselves, especially the dangerous spring work. Most jobs are completed in a single visit, and a broken spring is one of the most frequent emergency service calls a home generates.
dailyReality: You run a route of service calls out of a stocked van — often three to six jobs a day. A typical call means diagnosing the problem, quoting on the spot, and completing the repair while you are there: replacing a spring, restringing cables, swapping rollers, realigning track, or programming an opener. Spring work demands focus because torsion springs store significant energy and can injure you badly if released carelessly. Between calls you drive, take incoming calls, manage parts inventory in the van, and book the next jobs. Emergency calls and being reachable by phone drive a lot of the work, so responsiveness matters as much as wrench skill.

startupCostsItemized.item: Tools (winding bars, drills, levels, clamps, spring gauges, ladders)
Starting parts inventory (springs, cables, rollers, openers, remotes)
Business registration / LLC and any local contractor registration
Vehicle wrap or magnets and signage

realEarnings.yearOne: First-year solo operators who learn the work and market locally commonly earn $4,000 to $9,000 per month once calls are steady. Early months are slower while you build reviews and learn to diagnose and quote fast.
realEarnings.experienced: Operators with two or more years, strong reviews, and good call volume often report $9,000 to $18,000 per month solo or with one tech. Spring and opener jobs carry healthy margins, and add-on sales (full spring conversions, new openers, full door replacements) lift the average ticket.
realEarnings.topEarners: Multi-tech operations and those holding builder and property-manager accounts gross $30,000 or more per month, but reaching that means running several vans, dispatching, hiring techs you trust around dangerous spring work, and managing parts logistics. Most operators stay solo or run a small handful of vans.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Call volume and average ticket drive earnings most. Being reachable, responsive, and able to quote and close on the spot — plus honestly upselling worn parts that are about to fail — matters more than raw repair speed.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rates run roughly $75 to $200 per hour of on-site work because tickets are high and many repairs are quick, but driving between calls and phone time pull realistic blended rates down to $50 to $120 per hour.

howToStart: Before anything: Learn the work properly — especially torsion spring replacement, which is the dangerous part. Train through a manufacturer or distributor program, work under an experienced installer, or apprentice until you can change a spring safely and confidently. Do not learn spring tension from a video on a paying customer's door.

Week 1-2: Check local rules. Garage door work itself is unlicensed in many states, but some require a contractor or specialty registration, and electrical opener wiring may be regulated. Register the business and get general liability insurance before your first spring replacement or opener call.

Week 2-4: Outfit a van with the core tools and a starting parts inventory — common spring sizes, cables, rollers, remotes, and one or two opener models. Set up accounts with a garage door parts distributor so you can restock fast.

Month 1: Build a Google Business Profile, get listed where homeowners search for emergency repairs, and ask every customer for a review the day you finish. Same-day availability is your biggest early advantage over slower competitors.

Months 2-3: Pursue repeat referral sources — property managers, real estate agents, and small commercial accounts — and decide whether to add a second tech based on the call volume you are turning away.

mustHave: Mechanical aptitude and comfort with hand and power tools
Respect for and training in torsion spring tension safety — the non-negotiable core skill
Reliable diagnosis so you can identify the real problem and quote it on the first visit
Customer-facing communication to explain repairs and price them honestly on the spot
canLearn: Opener installation and programming across major brands
Full door replacement and panel work
Reading the specific spring size and cycle rating a door needs
separators: Fast, accurate diagnosis that lets you close jobs in one visit
Honest upselling of worn parts about to fail, which raises the average ticket without burning trust
Responsiveness and same-day availability that win the urgent emergency calls

whatPeopleGetWrong: Attempting torsion spring work without proper training and getting seriously injured by released tension
Carrying too little inventory, so they cannot complete the job on the first visit and lose the close
Quoting too low because they do not account for parts markup, drive time, and the value of same-day service
Being slow to answer the phone or book calls — the urgent jobs go to whoever responds first
Skipping general liability insurance on work that involves real injury and property-damage risk
Failing to spot and mention other worn parts, leaving money on the table and risking a quick callback

toolsAndEquipment.note: The right winding bars and a careful method are what make spring work safe — never improvise these.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Cordless drill, sockets, and impact driver
toolsAndEquipment.note: For brackets, track, and opener mounting. A reliable cordless setup speeds every job.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Ladders, clamps, levels, and vise grips
toolsAndEquipment.note: For holding doors and aligning track safely while you work.
Common springs, cables, rollers, hinges, remotes, and an opener or two so you finish on visit one.
To match the correct wire size, inside diameter, and length for a replacement spring.
Your shop on wheels. Shelving keeps parts organized so you are not digging on a customer's driveway.

methods: A strong Google Business Profile with reviews — most repair customers search 'garage door repair near me' in a hurry
Local service listings and pay-per-lead platforms for emergency repair calls
Property managers, landlords, and real estate agents who need fast, repeat service
Yard signs and van branding so a finished job advertises to the whole street
Asking every customer for a review immediately after a successful same-day fix
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Homeowners with a broken spring, dead opener, or off-track door who need it fixed today, plus property managers and small commercial sites with overhead doors. Most demand is urgent and search-driven.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Because the work is urgent and search-driven, operators can start booking calls within a few weeks of strong local listings and reviews. A steady, referral-fed pipeline usually builds over three to six months.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Generic brand advertising and social media before you have reviews. For this trade, ranking in local search and answering the phone fast beats almost any other marketing early on.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, and quickly for a capable operator, because tickets are high and demand is constant. A single busy van can produce a strong full-time income within the first year.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible, with a real caveat: you are hiring people to do dangerous spring work, so training and trust are critical. Operators who scale add vans and a dispatcher and standardize pricing and safety procedures before stepping off the tools.
howItScales.sellable: Established companies with multiple vans, a brand, reviews, and repeat commercial accounts sell for a multiple of profit plus vehicle and inventory value. A solo operation built entirely on the owner's reputation is harder to sell.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Multiple stocked vans, trained and trustworthy techs, a dispatch and scheduling system, distributor relationships for parts, and marketing that generates calls without your personal time.

strongFit: You are mechanically inclined and will train properly on spring tension safety
You like fast-paced, same-day work and being out on a route
You can quote and close jobs in person without overthinking it
You can be responsive and reachable when urgent calls come in
poorFit: You are unwilling to invest in real training and treat spring tension casually
You dislike sales and explaining or quoting repairs to homeowners
You cannot keep a van stocked and organized to finish jobs in one visit
You want a desk-based or fully passive business rather than driving a service route and turning wrenches on doors
askYourself: Have I trained enough to change a torsion spring safely, every time, under pressure?
Can I be responsive enough to win urgent same-day calls in my area?
Am I comfortable quoting and closing jobs on the spot, including honest upsells?

faqs.question: Do I need a license to repair garage doors?
faqs.answer: It varies. Many states do not require a specific garage door license, but some require a general or specialty contractor registration, and electrical work on openers can be regulated separately. Always confirm with your state contractor board and local permit office before taking paid work, and carry general liability insurance regardless.

faqs.question: How dangerous is garage door spring work, really?
faqs.answer: Torsion springs store a large amount of energy under tension, and releasing or installing them incorrectly can cause serious injuries to hands, face, and eyes. It is the single most important reason this is not a learn-on-the-job trade for paying customers. Get proper training — through a distributor program or an experienced installer — before you touch a customer's spring.

faqs.question: How much can I charge for a spring replacement?
faqs.answer: Spring replacements are commonly priced as a few hundred dollars per job including parts and labor, with two-spring conversions and high-cycle springs costing more. Openers and full door replacements run higher. Price from your parts cost, drive time, and the value of same-day service rather than racing competitors to the bottom.

faqs.question: Can I run this part-time around another job?
faqs.answer: To a degree, yes. The work is largely same-day and weekday-driven, and a part-time operator can take calls in the evenings and on weekends. But the urgent, search-driven nature of demand means you will lose many calls if you cannot respond promptly, which caps part-time volume.

faqs.question: How much inventory do I need to carry in the van?
faqs.answer: Enough to finish most jobs on the first visit — common spring sizes, cables, rollers, hinges, remotes, and at least one or two opener models. The whole economic advantage of the trade is completing the repair while you are there, so under-stocking quietly kills your close rate and your reviews.

faqs.question: Is the work seasonal?
faqs.answer: Less seasonal than outdoor trades — springs and openers fail year-round. Cold weather can actually increase failures as metal contracts and springs that were already worn finally break. Demand is steadier than weather-dependent trades like concrete or pressure washing.

faqs.question: What is the most common mistake new operators make?
faqs.answer: Two: treating spring tension casually before they are trained, and under-stocking the van so they cannot finish the job on the first visit. The first is a safety problem; the second quietly destroys profitability because re-visits cost you drive time and lose the close. Train properly and stock well.
